---
name: frontend-ui-engineering
description: "Builds production-quality UIs. Use when building or modifying user-facing interfaces. Use when creating components, implementing layouts, managing state, or when the output needs to look production-quality rather than AI-generated."
---

# Frontend UI Engineering

## Overview

Build production-quality user interfaces that are accessible, performant, and visually polished. The goal is UI that looks like it was built by a design-aware engineer — not like it was generated by AI.

## When to Use

- Building new UI components or pages
- Modifying existing user-facing interfaces
- Implementing responsive layouts
- Fixing visual or UX issues

## Component Architecture

- **Colocate** component, test, types, and hooks in the same directory
- **Composition over configuration** — composable children, not mega-prop components
- **Keep components focused** — each does one thing
- **Separate data fetching from presentation** — container + presentation split

## State Management

Choose the simplest approach:
- `useState` → Component-specific UI state
- Lifted state → Shared between 2-3 siblings
- Context → Theme, auth, locale (read-heavy, write-rare)
- URL state → Filters, pagination, shareable state
- Server state (React Query, SWR) → Remote data with caching
- Global store → Complex client state shared app-wide

Avoid prop drilling deeper than 3 levels.

## Design System Adherence

### Avoid the AI Aesthetic

| AI Default | Production Quality |
|---|---|
| Purple/indigo everything | Use the project's actual color palette |
| Excessive gradients | Flat or subtle per design system |
| Rounded everything | Consistent border-radius from the design system |
| Oversized padding | Consistent spacing scale |
| Generic hero sections | Content-first layouts |

- Use a consistent spacing scale (don't invent values)
- Respect the type hierarchy (h1 → h2 → h3, don't skip levels)
- Use semantic color tokens (`text-primary`, `bg-surface`), not raw hex

## Accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA)

- **Keyboard navigation:** Every interactive element must be keyboard accessible
- **ARIA labels:** Label interactive elements that lack visible text
- **Focus management:** Move focus when content changes (modals, dynamic content)
- **Meaningful states:** Handle loading, error, and empty states
- **Contrast:** 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text
- **Don't rely on color alone** to convey information

## Responsive Design

Design mobile-first, then expand. Test at: 320px, 768px, 1024px, 1440px.

## Loading and Transitions

- Use skeleton loading (not spinners) for content
- Use optimistic updates for perceived speed
- Add `aria-busy="true"` to loading regions

## Common Rationalizations

| Rationalization | Reality |
|---|---|
| "Accessibility is a nice-to-have" | It's a legal requirement and engineering quality standard |
| "We'll make it responsive later" | Retrofitting responsive design is 3x harder |
| "The AI aesthetic is fine for now" | It signals low quality. Use the project's design system. |

## Red Flags

- Components with more than 200 lines
- Inline styles or arbitrary pixel values
- Missing error, loading, or empty states
- No keyboard navigation testing
- Color as the sole indicator of state

## Verification

- [ ] Component renders without console errors
- [ ] All interactive elements are keyboard accessible
- [ ] Screen reader can convey content and structure
- [ ] Responsive: works at 320px, 768px, 1024px, 1440px
- [ ] Loading, error, and empty states handled
- [ ] Follows project's design system
